coredata.dc:title: Biochar coupling induced charge transfer switching of a NiAl2O4/NiCr2O4 photocatalyst to enhance photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride

coredata.dc:description: A series of novel (NiAl2O4 (NAO)/NiCr2O4 (NCO)) (S1)/GL heterojunction photocatalysts, denoting as x%GL/S1 (x = 5, 10, 15 and 20), were prepared by the polyacrylamide gel method combined with low temperature sintering technique. Various characterizations confirm that there is special interfacial contact and charge transfer between NAO, NCO and GL. The 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction exhibited the best photocatalytic activity and cyclic stability for the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC). Compared to NAO, NCO and NAO/NCO, the degradation efficiency of 10 %GL/S1 was significantly improved, and the degradation percentage reached 83.2 % within 30 min. The activity of 10 %GL/S1 was studied in relation to catalyst content, drug concentration, pH value, water quality, and co-existing anion. Trapping experiments and free radical verification experiments confirmed that the holes, hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals played crucial roles in the photocatalysis process. Possible pathways for TC degradation were studied by HPLC-MS, and the ecotoxicity of TC and its degradation intermediates was evaluated by T.E.S.T. software. The charge transfer and photocatalytic mechanisms confirm that the introduction of GL enhances the interfacial interaction between the components, promotes the carrier migration efficiency, provides a large number of active sites for 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction, and produces active free radicals after redox reaction with photoinduced electrons, effectively improving the degradation efficiency of TC.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of novel (NiAl2O4 (NAO)/NiCr2O4 (NCO)) (S1)/GL heterojunction photocatalysts, denoting as x%GL/S1 (x = 5, 10, 15 and 20), were prepared by the polyacrylamide gel method combined with low temperature sintering technique. Various characterizations confirm that there is special interfacial contact and charge transfer between NAO, NCO and GL. The 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction exhibited the best photocatalytic activity and cyclic stability for the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC). Compared to NAO, NCO and NAO/NCO, the degradation efficiency of 10 %GL/S1 was significantly improved, and the degradation percentage reached 83.2 % within 30 min. The activity of 10 %GL/S1 was studied in relation to catalyst content, drug concentration, pH value, water quality, and co-existing anion. Trapping experiments and free radical verification experiments confirmed that the holes, hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals played crucial roles in the photocatalysis process. Possible pathways for TC degradation were studied by HPLC-MS, and the ecotoxicity of TC and its degradation intermediates was evaluated by T.E.S.T. software. The charge transfer and photocatalytic mechanisms confirm that the introduction of GL enhances the interfacial interaction between the components, promotes the carrier migration efficiency, provides a large number of active sites for 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction, and produces active free radicals after redox reaction with photoinduced electrons, effectively improving the degradation efficiency of TC.

A novel NiAl2O4/NiCr2O4/ginkgo leaf (GL) heterojunction photocatalysts were synthesized by the polyacrylamide gel method combined with a low temperature sintering technique. The NiAl2O4/NiCr2O4/GL photocatalyst exhibits high photocatalytic activity and cyclic stability for the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As pharmaceutical science and technology progress, antibiotics have become a type of drug that people rely on because of their excellent antibacterial properties and applications. To treat diseases, promote plant and animal growth, and facilitate aquaculture, a variety of antibiotics have been utilized in recent times [1]. Among them, tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) is a common broad-spectrum antibiotic, often used in the treatment of non-bacterial infections such as chlamydia infection, but also used in the treatment of brucellosis, cholera and respiratory system, urinary tract infections caused by sensitive bacteria [2]. However, since antibiotics can only be absorbed by a small part of people or animals, most of them will flow into nature in the form of excrement through people and animals, causing damage to the environment and water quality. To ensure the survival rate and success rate of aquatic products, antibiotics are commonly used in their construction along rivers [3]. Antibiotic pollution in water can be effectively controlled by degrading antibiotics from the source. Traditional water treatment technology can cause secondary pollution of the water body in either a costly or easy manner [4]. Therefore, the development of environmentally friendly, green, safe and efficient degradation technologies to degrade antibiotics has become a top priority.

Advanced oxidation technology (AOP) has recently been identified as a promising technology for reducing pollutants. Photocatalysis technology is a type of AOP technology that provides a green and effective approach to degrade antibiotics and hydrogen production [5-10]. Choosing the right photocatalyst is essential for photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics. Traditional photocatalysts have drawbacks such as high electron and hole recombination efficiency, large band gap, and poor visible light response that limit their use in photocatalysis. It is still a significant challenge to choose photocatalysts that are reasonable and stable, with high degradation efficiency. Recently, semiconductor-based heterojunction photocatalysts have been widely used in the field of photocatalysis due to their safety, sustainability and environmental friendliness [11-13]. There are many research groups that focus on the development of heterojunction photocatalysts that have exceptional properties that can be utilized for the efficient degradation of antibiotics.

Spinel oxide (AB2O4), a typical semiconductor material, has been commonly utilized in photocatalysis due to its stable structure, straightforward synthesis, and low cost. Where, A represents a + 2-valent cation that includes Mg, Cu, Ni, Zn, Fe, or other metallic elements, while B represents a + 3-valent cation that includes Al, Cr, or Fe, among others. Common spinel oxide photocatalysts mainly including NiAl2O4 [14], MgAl2O4 [15], NiCr2O4 [16], CaCr2O4 [12], ZnFe2O4 [17], and CuFe2O4 [18], etc. Among them, the spinel nickel aluminate (NiAl2O4, NAO) presents a partial inverse spinel structure. The +3-valent cation in the crystal structure mainly occupies the tetrahedral structure, while the +2-valent and other +3-valent cations are filling in the octahedron. The high thermal stability, high melting point, high charge transfer and separation efficiency, and low surface acidity make it commonly used in catalysts and refractories. The special energy band structure and good redox potential of NAO make it ideal for wastewater treatment applications [19]. The optical band gap value of around 2.8 - 3 eV hinders its visible light response ability, limiting its application in the field of photocatalysis to a certain extent. Through the concepts of heterojunction and co-catalysis, the problems of poor visible light response and high charge-carrier recombination rate can be effectively solved by continuous advancement of technology. The photocatalytic activity of the system can be effectively enhanced by coupling the two spinel structure materials together to form a heterojunction with special interface contact [20]. Nickel chromate (NiCr2O4, NCO), as a typical p-type semiconductor, has a narrow band gap, generally in the range of 2 - 2.5 eV, has a high charge carrier transfer and separation efficiency, and can respond to visible light [21]. The combination of NCO and a wide-band gap photocatalyst can enhance the system's response to visible light. The promise of fascinating physicochemical properties can be achieved by combining NAO and NCO to form a heterojunction, but no researchers have yet constructed an NAO/NCO heterojunction and studied its physicochemical properties.

Biochar-based materials are advantageous in terms of their easy preparation, large specific surface area, abundant functional groups, porous structure, and high visible light utilization rate [22]. The transfer of electrons in the heterojunction photocatalyst system is facilitated by biochar-based materials, which effectively reduces the recombination efficiency of electron hole pairs. Ginkgo leaf (GL) powder is a biochar-based material that can serve as an excellent electron carrier and transport charge in heterojunction photocatalysts [23-25]. GL was utilized by Huang et al. to make biomass powder and created a new Bi2O3/GL/g-C3N4 Z-scheme heterojunction for TC degradation [26]. The findings showed that conductive biochar had a beneficial effect on the degradation of pollutants by enhancing the rate of electron transfer and photoinduced carrier separation. Cheng and his colleagues established a GL-C3N4 heterojunction by utilizing GL powder to degrade Rhodamine B (RhB) [27]. According to the findings, GL is responsible for exciton separation and charge transfer in photocatalysis, which greatly enhances the catalytic performance of the GL-C3N4 heterojunction. GL has the ability to transform waste into valuable resources by using local materials. The introduction of GL into NAO/NCO heterojunction can lead to an improvement in charge transfer and separation efficiency between surfaces and interfaces of GL and NAO/NCO, which is an intriguing research topic that has yet to be reported.

Various heterojunctions can be constructed using the polyacrylamide gel method combined with low temperature sintering techniques, such as MgFe2O4/Fe2O3/CoCr2O4 [28], g-C3N4/Au/MgAl2O4 [29], CQDs/CeO2/PbFe12O19 [30], YMnO3/MgAl2O4 [31] and others. In this paper, a series of NAO/NCO/GL (x%GL/S1) heterojunction photocatalysts were prepared by the polyacrylamide gel method and low temperature sintering technology. The preparation materials were thoroughly characterized in detail, with special focus on the phase structure, functional groups, charge states, microstructure, optical properties, and photocatalytic activity. Through microstructure characterization, the special surface and interface contacts of x%GL/S1 heterojunction were explored, which is helpful to further explore the photocatalytic mechanism of x%GL/S1 heterojunction. The photocatalytic activity of x%GL/S1 was studied by using TC as the target degradation pollutant. The result shows that GL effectively transferred photogenerated electrons to NAO/NCO, prevented electron hole pairs from recombining, and improved the degradation efficiency of TC by enhancing recombination efficiency. A systematic evaluation was conducted to examine the impact of environmental factors such as catalyst content, drug concentration, pH, water, and co-existing anions on the photocatalytic activity of 10 % GL/S1 heterojunction. Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) and toxicity evaluation software were used to investigate and evaluate the degradation pathway of TC and the toxicity of its intermediate products. Based on band arrangement theory, free radical verification experiment, work function calculation and charge transfer mechanism, a reasonable photocatalytic mechanism of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction has been proposed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NCO and NAO photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The NiCr2O4 (NCO) and NiAl2O4 (NAO) nanoparticles were prepared by the polyacrylamide gel method. The preparation process of NCO nanoparticles is as follows: According to the stoichiometric ratio of NCO, 1.3142 g NiSO4·6H2O (98.5 %) and 4.0018 g Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (99.0 %) were dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water. Under continuous magnetic stirring, the reagent is fully dissolved in water. Then, moderate chelating agent (citric acid, 99.5 % AR), anti-gel collapse agent (glucose, 99.0 % AR), acrylamide (99.0 % AR) and N, N '-methylene bisacrylamide (99.0 % AR) were added to the above solution successively. After stirring for 2 h, the temperature is raised to 90 °C. The molded gel was transferred to a drying oven and dried at 120 °C for 24 h to obtain a black xerogel. Finally, the xerogel was ground and roasted in a box furnace at 1000 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C /min to obtain the required powder, which was recorded as NCO. Similarly, the preparation process of NAO nanoparticles is consistent with that of NCO. The preparation process of NAO nanoparticles is described in detail in Section S1 of the Supporting information file. The schematic delineating of the preparation process of NCO and NAO as shown in Fig. 1 .

NAO and NCO were weighed according to the mass percents of NAO: NCO = 95 wt%: 5 wt%, 90 wt%: 10 wt%, 85 wt%: 15 wt%, and 80 wt%: 20 wt%. Place the weighed NAO and NCO separately in a mortar, mix thoroughly and grind for 1 h. The ground powder was transferred to a tube furnace and sintered at 200 °C for 2 h to obtain 95 wt%NAO/NCO, 90 wt%NAO/NCO, 85 wt%NAO/NCO and 80 wt%NAO/NCO photocatalysts, which were named S1, S2, S3 and S4, respectively. The corresponding preparation flow chart as shown in Fig. 1.

The ginkgo leaf in the experiment came from the beautiful campus of Chongqing Three Gorges University, which has many ginkgo trees. First, ginkgo biloba leaves were washed multiple times with H2O and ethanol to remove impurities and chlorophyll. The cleaned ginkgo biloba leaves were transferred to a drying oven and dried at 60 °C for 4 h to obtain the dried ginkgo biloba leaves. The dried ginkgo leaves are crushed into a powder through a mortar. Subsequently, the powder is transferred to a box furnace and sintered at 700 °C for 5 h to obtain the required powder, referred to as GL.

Samples S1 and GL obtained by the above process were used to prepare NAO/NCO/GL photocatalysts. The main purpose of selecting S1 is to perform photocatalytic experiments and find that it has the best photocatalytic effect. First, according to the mass percentage of S1: GL= 95 wt%: 5 wt%, 90 wt%: 10 wt%, 85 wt%: 15 wt%, 80 wt%: 20 wt%, weigh the appropriate amount of S1 and GL into the mortar and mix thoroughly. After fully mixing and grinding for 1 h, the mixed powder is transferred to a tubular furnace. After sintering at 200 °C for 2 h, (95 wt%NAO/NCO)/5 wt% GL, (95 wt%NAO/NCO)/10 wt% GL, (95 wt%NAO/NCO)/15 wt% GL, and (95 wt%NAO/NCO)/20 wt% GL photocatalysts are obtained. The (95 wt%NAO/NCO)/5 wt% GL, (95 wt%NAO/NCO)/10 wt% GL, (95 wt%NAO/NCO)/15 wt% GL, and (95 wt%NAO/NCO)/20 wt% GL photocatalysts are recorded as 5 %GL/S1, 10 %GL/S1, 15 %GL/S1 and 20 %GL/S1, respectively.

The phase structure and purity, chemical groups and surface functional groups, chemical valence states and electron transfer mechanism, surface morphology and lattice information, optical properties, the electron spin resonance (ESR)/electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments, TC intermediates and degradation pathways of the NCO, x %NAO/NCO, GL and x %GL/S1 photocatalysts was characterized by multiple characterization equipment. Details of the experimental parameters are described in Section S2 of the supporting information file.

The photocatalytic activities of NCO, NAO, x %NAO/NCO, GL and x %GL/S1 photocatalysts were evaluated using TC as the target degradation pollutant. The photocatalytic experiments are performed using a photochemical reactor produced by Porfilet Technologies LTD. 300 W xenon lamp is used as a light source to simulate sunlight. First, 1 g/L photocatalyst was added to 50 mL TC solution and continuously stirred in a dark environment for 30 min to achieve adsorption resolution equilibrium. Subsequently, a mixture of photocatalyst and TC solution was placed in the system under simulated solar light irradiation for photocatalytic reaction. During the reaction process, 2–3 mL suspension is collected every 5 min, and the required liquid is filtered immediately after the full reaction is completed. The absorbance of the liquid was measured with UV-1800 UV–visible spectrophotometer at 357 nm, and the catalytic activity of the photocatalyst was more directly investigated with the help of kinetic curve. Eqs. (1) and (2) were used to calculate the degradation percentage (DP) and the first-order kinetic curve of the photocatalyst.
(1)

Where, A0 is the absorbance of the initial concentration of TC, At is the absorbance of the TC solution at time t, and k is the first-order kinetic constant (min-1). To study the influence of different environmental parameters on the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst, the catalyst content, drug concentration and the pH value of the reaction solution were 0.5 - 2 g/L, 25 - 100 mg/L and 3 - 11, respectively. In the process of studying the influence of environmental parameters on the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst, only the parameters that need to be explored are changed, while other experimental conditions remain unchanged. The effect of pH on TC degradation efficiency was evaluated by adjusting the pH value of TC solution with NaOH (97 %) and HNO3 (AR).

The cyclic stability of the photocatalyst was studied by five cycles under the same experimental conditions. After the photocatalytic reaction, the remaining solution is filtered to obtain the residual catalyst. After drying treatment for 3 h, the dried catalyst was placed in a tubular furnace sintered at 200 °C for 2 h to remove residual TC molecules and then the next cycle experiment was performed. For the capture experiment, 2-propanol (IPA, 99.7 % AR, 1 mmol/L), 1,4-Benzoquinone (BQ, 97.0 % AR, 1 mmol/L) and ethyl-diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na, 99.0 % AR, 1 mmol/L) were used as trapping agents to study the effects of •OH, •O2 − and holes on the photocatalytic activity. 1 mmol/L of NaCl (99.5 % AR), Na2SO4 (99.0 % AR), NaHCO3 (99.5 % AR) and NaH2PO4 (99.0 % AR) were used as trapping agents to evaluate the effects of co-existing anions on TC degradation efficiency.

Based on first principles density functional theory (DFT), the work function information of NAO and NCO was obtained by Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) [32]. All calculations were processed using the Perdew Burke Ernzerhof (PBE) functional within the General gradient approximation (GGA). The (311) and (211) crystal faces were selected for the NAO and NCO, and a vacuum layer of 10 Å thickness was set along the Z direction to avoid the influence of periodic boundary conditions on the crystal faces. The truncation energy of the plane wave is set to 480 eV to calculate the total energy of the structure. For all geometric relaxations, the convergence criteria for energy and force are set to 1 × 10–5 eV and 0.05 eV/Å, respectively. In the calculation of work function, a 1 × 1 × 1 K-point sampling grid is used in Brillouin region. In addition, spin polarization is taken into account in all calculations.

All photocatalytic degradation experiments were repeated three times. The average value of all tests is considered and an error bar is provided. The Origin 2021 software was used to map all the data. The first-order dynamical behavior and the associated fitting analysis were performed using Origin 2021 software.

XRD was used to characterize the crystal structure of all samples and obtain the corresponding phase purity information. In Fig. 2 a, the main diffraction peaks of NCO at 2θ = 18.473°, 30.283°, 35.987°, 37.450°, 43.834°, 54.204°, 57.960°, 62.987°, 75.073° and 79.890° correspond to the (101), (112), (211), (202), (220), (312), (321), (224), (413) and (404) crystal planes of tetragonal NCO, respectively. These values correspond to the crystal data of the NCO standard card No. PDF # 88–0109. However, a few weak diffraction peaks were observed in the XRD pattern of NCO. This observed peaks at 2θ = 24.498°, 33.606°, 41.478°, 50.215° and 54.850° correspond to the (110), (121), (120), (220) and (231) crystal planes of trigonal Cr2O3 with the standard card No. PDF # 85–0730, respectively. This result indicates that a small amount of Cr2O3 is present in NCO. In the XRD pattern of x%NAO/NCO composites, the corresponding diffraction peaks of NAO and NCO can be clearly observed. The intensities of diffraction peaks at 18.473°, 30.283°, 37.450° and 62.987° increase with the increasing of NCO content. Simultaneously, the position of the diffraction peak shifted slightly with the increase of NCO content, indicating that there is a strong interaction between NAO and NCO. This conclusion suggests that a heterojunction is formed between NAO and NCO, but further experiments are needed to confirm it. It is worth noting that there is no diffraction peak of Cr2O3 in the binary composite, which may be due to the low NCO content.

Fig. 2b displays the XRD patterns of GL and x%GL/S1 photocatalysts. In Figure S1, the major diffraction peaks of GL are associated with calcium carbonate (PDF # 86–2341), Ca(PO4)2 (PDF # 86–1585), CaSO4 (PDF # 03–0377) and C60 (PDF # 79–1715), indicate that GL may contain large amounts of calcium-based substances, especially calcium carbonate in the calcite structure. The XRD pattern of x%GL/S1 (Fig. 2b) clearly observed the diffraction peaks of GL, NAO and NCO, especially the diffraction peaks belonging to GL in the range of 28 - 34° With the increase of GL proportion, the diffraction peak intensity also increases gradually, indicating that GL is successfully decorated on S1 surface. No other impurity peaks were found in the XRD pattern, indicating that the addition of GL did not change the phase structure of x%NAO/NCO.

The chemical structures and surface functional groups of GL, NCO, x%NAO/NCO and x%GL/S1 photocatalysts were characterized by FTIR technique. As shown in Fig. 2c, NCO and x%NAO/NCO have a wide absorption band at 3456 cm-1 due to the stretching vibration of water molecules [33]. An obvious characteristic peak is located at 1636 cm-1, possibly due to the bending vibration of adsorbed water [34]. These results indicate that the sample may adsorb a small amount of water molecules during the preservation process. It is well known that the characteristic peaks of metal oxides are in the range of 450–800 cm-1. Therefore, the characteristic peaks of NAO and NCO can be clearly observed in the FTIR spectra of x%NAO/NCO. For NCO, the characteristic peaks at 517 and 617 cm-1 correspond to the vibrations of Cr-O (octahedron) and Ni-O (tetrahedron), respectively [35,36]. For NAO, the peaks at 507 and 724 cm-1 correspond to the vibrations of Al-O (octahedron) and Ni-O-Al, respectively, indicating that NCO successfully loads into NAO [37].

There are many characteristic peaks in the FTIR spectrum of GL as shown in Fig. 2d. Among them, the peaks at 1636 and 3180–3680 cm-1 are caused by bending and stretching vibrations of water molecules, respectively, which are similar to the previous samples. The peak at 1536 cm-1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the C=C bond, indicating the existence of the aromatic ring [38]. There are obvious characteristic peaks at 1360–1471 cm-1, which are related to the CC bond vibration of CO3 2- [39,40]. Since organic calcium is gradually converted to CaCO3 during pyrolysis, the signals at 874 and 1021 cm-1 correspond to the out-of-plane bending vibration of CO3 2- in calcite (CaCO3) [41,42]. This is consistent with the XRD pattern of GL, indicating that there is a small amount of CaCO3 in GL. The characteristic peak at 1049 and 1087 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO bond, and the signal at 1126 cm-1 is related to the stretching vibration of CO-C bond [43-45]. The XRD pattern shows that a small amount of Ca(PO4)2 is present in GL, so a weak signal at 962 cm-1 corresponds to a symmetric stretching of PO4 3- (v1) [46]. It can be observed from the FTIR spectra of x%GL/S1 ternary composite that the metal characteristic peaks of NAO and NCO appear at 507, 617 and 724 cm-1. In addition, the characteristic peak of GL also appears in the FTIR spectrum of x%GL/S1, indicating a successful coupling between GL and S1.

The charge states and elemental compositions of GL, NCO, S1 and 10 %GL/S1 photocatalysts were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and the binding energy data of all elements were shown in Table S1-S8. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the full spectrum of NCO includes the characteristic peaks corresponding to elements Ni, Cr, O and C, while GL contains elements C, O, Ca, S and P. In Fig. 3b, NAO/NCO contains elements Ni, Al, O, Cr, and C, while 10 %GL/S1 contains elements C, O, Ca, S, P, Ni, Al, and Cr. This result is consistent with that of FTIR analysis. In the C1s spectrum of all samples, the C=C bond of 284.80 eV was used to correct the binding energy of the other elements. Here, the XPS analysis of NCO is shown in Figure S2a-d and Section S4 in Supporting information file. For GL (Fig. 3c), the binding energies at 286.23, 288.74, and 289.44 eV represent CO, OC=O, and π-π∗, respectively. According to the results of XRD pattern, GL contains a certain amount of CaCO3 and Ca(PO4)2, so the characteristic peak of lattice oxygen (531.01 eV) appears in the high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 3d). In addition, the binding energies at 531.84 and 533.55 eV correspond to -OH and C=O, respectively. Fig. 3e shows the high resolution spectrum of Ca 2p, with binding energies at 346.80 and 350.39 eV corresponding to Ca 2p3/2 and Ca 2p1/2 orbits, respectively, and its satellite peaks corresponding to 348.55 and 351.78 eV [4,47]. As shown in Fig. 3f, two typical peaks occur in the high-resolution spectrum of P 2p, corresponding to the P 2p3/2 (132.80 eV) and P 2p1/2 (133.74 eV) orbits [48]. Notably, compared with GL, the P 2p spectrum of 10 %GL/S1 moved slightly towards lower binding energy (0.1 eV), indicating an increasing electron concentration in GL. Fig. 3g shows the S 2p deconvolution spectrum, where two obvious characteristic peaks are observed at 166.34 and 168.60 eV, representing the 2p3/2 orbits of S4+ and S6+, respectively. In addition, the 2p1/2 orbits of S4+ and S6+ correspond to binding energies at 167.14 and 169.80 eV, respectively [49]. Electron interactions are possible in 10 %GL/S1 systems due to a slight blue shift in the deconvolution S 2p of 10 %GL/S1.

As shown in Fig. 3h, the Ni 2p spectrum of S1 is almost consistent with NAO and NCO except for Ni0+, which is attributed to the lower NCO content. In addition, the Ni 2p spectra of 10 %GL/S1 show a slight redshift compared to S1, indicating a gradual decrease in the electron density of S1. Tetrahedral and octahedral of Al3+ appear in the Al 2p spectrum of S1 and 10 %GL/S1 (Fig. 3i), indicating that NAO and NCO are successfully coupled. Fig. 3j shows the O 1 s spectra of S1 and 10 %GL/S1, and oxygen vacancy appears in both composite samples, indicating that a large number of oxygen vacancies are generated in the composite materials by using this experimental method. These oxygen vacancies help to enhance the absorption of visible light, accelerate the transfer of photogenerated charge, and make 10 %GL/S1 have excellent photocatalytic activity [50]. Fig. 3k shows the Cr 2p deconvolution spectrum of S1. The binding energies at 575.76 and 577.71 eV correspond to the 2p 3/2 orbits of Cr3+ and Cr6+, respectively, and the peaks at 584.99 and 587.43 eV correspond to the 2p1/2 orbits of Cr3+ and Cr6+ [51]. Compared to S1, a slight change in the position of Cr 2p deconvolution spectrum of 10 %GL/S1 can be clearly observed. This slight redshift indicates that charge redistribution is taking place in the composite [52]. Finally, Fig. 3l shows that the C 1 s spectra of S1 and 10 %GL/S1 have similar characteristic peaks. For S1, the binding energies at 286.27 and 288.26 eV corresponds to CO and OC=O, respectively. For 10 %GL/S1, the characteristic peaks of 285.85, 288.45, and 289.55 eV represent CO, OC=O, and π-π∗, respectively, due to the large amount of C element in GL.

The interfacial interaction of heterojunction can be analyzed according to the migration of XPS binding energy. Compared with 10 %GL/S1, the fitting peaks of Ni 2p, Cr 2p and O 1 s of S1 all move towards the higher binding energy, indicating that S1 component has a lower electron concentration. On the contrary, the P 2p and S 2p spectra in 10 %GL/S1 are slightly blue shifted compared with GL, indicating that the electron concentration of GL component is increasing. Therefore, the change in binding energy indicates that there is an interaction between GL and S1, which is manifested by the transfer of electrons from S1 to GL. This conclusion provides important experimental support for the subsequent analysis of the photocatalytic mechanism of 10 %GL/S1 photocatalyst.

The surface microstructure of NCO, GL, S1 and 10 %GL/S1 photocatalysts were characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 4 a, NCO presents as some rhombus particles of different sizes. The surface of these particles is smooth and retains some gaps, which can provide more active sites. As can be seen from Fig. 4b, GL consists of many small spherical particles with smooth surfaces, which is similar to the SEM images of ginkgo biloba leaf powder previously studied [53,54]. It can also be seen from the figure that there is an obvious adhesion and agglomeration phenomenon among the particles. This adhesion agglomeration will cause TC molecules to be unable to adsorb well on GL surface to some extent. Previous studies have shown that NAO comes in the form of spherical particles with a particle size of about 10 nm [55]. With the introduction of NCO, the morphology of NAO did not change, and it still showed the shape of tightly packed spherical small particles, as shown in Fig. 4c. Similarly, the topography of 10 %GL/S1 (Fig. 4d) is similar to S1, indicating that the introduction of GL does not significantly change the topography of S1. It is worth noting that the large rhomboid particles are not observed in Fig. 4c and d mainly because the content of NCO is relatively small, and most of the fine particles of NAO may cover the surface of NCO.

Fig. 5 a displays the TEM image of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunctions. As shown in Fig. 5a, the 10 %GL/S1 photocatalyst is mainly composed of some spherical fine particles and rhomboidal large particles. According to SEM observation, the fine particles are NAO or GL, while the large rhomboid particles are NCO. Similarly, particle-to-particle adhesion and agglomeration were evident, indicating that the NAO, NCO, and GL formed special interfacial contacts. Fig. 5b shows the HRTEM images of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunctions corresponding to the selected area. Three clear lattice fringes appear in the 10 %GL/S1 composite with the spacing of 0.24, 0.25 and 0.32 nm, corresponding to (311) plane of NAO, (211) plane of NCO and (024) plane of GL, respectively. The interfacial contact between particles is very obvious, indicating the formation of heterojunctions between NAO, NCO and GL. Fig. 5c-k display the BRTEM and the corresponding element mapping images of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunctions. The results show that the 10 %GL/S1 composites contain only Ni, Al, O, Cr, C, Ca, P and S elements. Among them, Ni, Al, Cr, and O mainly come from NAO/NCO heterojunction, while C, Ca, P, and S mainly belong to GL. All elements are evenly distributed on the NAO matrix, further confirming the formation of special heterostructures between NAO, NCO and GL. The EDS spectrum of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunctions as shown in Fig. 5l. All elements are contained in NAO, NCO or GL, and do not contain any other impurity elements.

The optical properties of NCO and a series of composite samples were studied by UV–Vis diffuse reflection and absorption spectra. Fig. 6 a displays the UV–vis diffuse reflection spectrum of NCO, S1, S2, S3 and S4. The reflectance of the sample increases with the increasing of wavelength in the four ranges of 210–230, 290–350, 400–520 and 670–900 nm. In contrast, the reflectance decreases significantly with the increasing of wavelength in the ranges of 190–210 and 350–410 nm. With the increase of NCO content, the reflection of NAO/NCO photocatalyst increases first and then decreases, and Sample S3 has the largest reflectance. This phenomenon is different from the principle of mechanistic spectrum superposition principle. [56] The anomalous reflectivity of NAO/NCO photocatalyst may be due to only a small fraction of the reflectance information of NAO can be recorded in UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra. It is noted that the DRS spectrum of x%GL/S1 (Fig. 6b) is similar to that of x%NAO/NCO except that the reflectance values are different. For comparison, the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of GL is also given. The reflectance of GL fluctuates twice between 190 and 220 and 220–400 nm, until the reflectance increases with the increasing of wavelength after 400 nm. The reflectance of x%GL/S1 heterojunction decreases first and then increases with the increasing of GL content, and the reflectance of 15 %GL/S1 heterojunction is the least.

The color related parameters (L*, a*, b*, c*, Ho, ΔECIE*) of all samples are calculated based on the diffuse reflectance spectrum, and the details of the calculation process are given in Section S7 of the supporting information file. Table S9 displays the color related parameters of NCO, GL, S1, S2, S3, S4 and x%GL/S1 (x = 5, 10, 15, 20) heterojunctions. The a* value of NCO is negative, indicating the presence of a green component in the sample. For x%NAO/NCO (x = 5, 10, 15, 20), the increasing order of a* values is S3 > S2 > S4 > S1, indicating that S1 has the least green component. However, GL has high L* values and positive a* and b* values, indicating that GL is white. Similarly, the x%GL/S1 heterojunction has a negative a* value, indicating that the catalyst also contains a green component. It can also be seen from the table that the Sample S1 and 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction have relatively large L* values, which will provide experimental basis for the subsequent analysis of the photocatalytic mechanism. Figure S3 displays the real color photos of NCO, GL, S1, S2 and x%GL/S1 (x = 5, 10, 15, 20) heterojunctions. The calculated results are consistent with the color of the real photos.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of all samples can be obtained by using Kubelka-Munk formula (3).

Where, R, α and S are the reflectance, the absorption coefficient and the scattering coefficient of the prepared photocatalysts, respectively. The cationic coordination and oxidation state of the prepared samples can be studied according to the ultraviolet absorption spectrum. Fig. 6c and d display the UV-visible absorbance spectra of NCO, S1, S2, S3 and S4, GL and x%GL/S1 (x = 5, 10, 15, 20) heterojunctions. For the NAO/NCO heterojunctions, six obvious absorption peaks at 212, 290, 405, 634, 670 and 820 nm can be observed. The peaks at 212 and 290 nm can be assigned to the charge transfer of O2- → Ni2+ [57]. The peaks at 405 nm can be ascribed to the octahedral coordination of Ni2+ including charge transfer bands and 3A2g–3T1g spin allowing transition. The peaks at about 634 and 670 nm can be ascribed to the Ni2+ in octahedral sites and tetrahedral sites of the 3T1–3T2 (3P) spin allowing transition due to the NAO is an inverted spinel structure, respectively [58]. A similar characteristic peak was observed at 820 nm, but it was not explained [58]. In this experiment, the absorption peak at 820 nm may be caused by the interface defect when NAO and NCO are coupled. It is worth noting that all characteristic peaks shift significantly relative to NAO, which is mainly due to the change of absorption peak position after NCO coupling. The results show that the special heterojunction is formed at the interface of NAO and NCO. In addition to the characteristic peaks of NAO/NCO heterojunction for the GL and x%GL/S1 (x = 5, 10, 15, 20) heterojunctions, the characteristic peaks at 210 and 274 nm of GL can be found. The peaks at 210 and 274 nm can be ascribed to the π-π transition of aromatic CC bonds [59-61], the absorption of an aromatic p system or the p-π* transition of the carbonyl, respectively [62-64]. The results confirm the presence of a large number of CC bonds and CO functional groups in the x%GL/S1 heterojunction, suggesting the presence of carbonate in addition to carbon, which is consistent with the results of XRD, FTIR and XPS analysis.

According to the Tauc formula, the optical band gap (Eg) values of all samples can be calculated. The Tauc formula can be expressed by the Eq. (4).
(4)

Where, E = hν is the photon energy, and A is a proportionality constant. Generally, NCO appears as a direct band gap semiconductor [22,65], where n = 2. According to formula (4), the relationship curves between (αhν)2 and hν of all photocatalysts are obtained, as shown in Figure S4a and b. The Eg values of NCO, GL, S1, S2, S3, S4 and x%GL/S1 (x = 5, 10, 15, 20) heterojunctions are also given in Table S9. In our early experiments, the Eg value of NAO was about 2.92 eV [55]. The Eg value of NCO calculated in this experiment is 2.12 eV. Generally, the coupling of two semiconductor materials does not change the Eg value of the main lattice material. However, it can be seen from Table S9 that the Eg values of Samples S1-S4 are close to NCO, which may be caused by charge transfer during the formation of heterojunction between NAO and NCO. When GL is introduced into the NAO/NCO heterojunction, the Eg values of some samples are further reduced, indicating that GL has a great influence on the interface of NAO/NCO. This special interface contact makes each component have a strong charge transfer ability, which contributes to the photocatalytic ability of the system.

The photocatalytic activity of NCO, GL, x%NAO/NCO and x%GL/S1 heterojunctions under simulated sunlight irradiation was studied using TC solution as the target degradation pollutant. Before the photoreaction, the TC solution was stirred in a dark environment for 30 min to achieve adsorption resolution equilibrium. As can be seen from Figure S5a, the degradation efficiency of pure NCO is weak, and the removal rate is only 24.5 % after 30 min irradiation, which may be caused by the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron (e-) and hole (h +). Under the same photocatalytic experimental conditions, the degradation percentage of GL reached 52 %, indicating that the GL has a high charge carrier transfer and separation efficiency than that of NCO. Figure S5(b) shows the emission spectra of GL, NAO, S1 and 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction. It can be seen from the figure that the NAO, S1 and 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction have obvious emission peaks at 350 and 392 nm due to the surface defects. [58] However, the fluorescence emission peak of GL showed a significant shift near 392 nm, and a new fluorescence emission peak appeared at 465 nm. Comparing the intensity of emission peaks of all samples, the weakest GL implies that it has the highest charge transfer and separation efficiency. Although it has the highest charge transfer and separation efficiency, its photocatalytic degradation percentage is lower than that of sample S1 mainly because the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst is also related to the active species in the reaction solution. Earlier literature showed that after 30 min of illumination under the same experimental conditions, the TC removal rate of pure NAO was 61 %, which may be because NAO has a wide Eg value, which limits its light response ability [55]. Meanwhile, the rate of electron and hole transfer and separation in NAO is low, which makes NAO exhibit relatively poor photocatalytic activity. When NAO is coupled with NCO, its degradation efficiency is similar to that of pure NAO. As the proportion of NCO increased from 5 % to 15 %, its photocatalytic efficiency gradually decreased, from 61.7 % to 49.9 %. This may be because the traditional type I heterojunction inhibits the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron hole pairs. It is worth noting that the photocatalytic efficiency increases slightly to 59.9 % when the mass percentage is 20 %, which may be due to experimental errors and the charge transfer ability between the NAO and NCO interfaces.

Fig. 7 a disiplays the degradation efficiencies of NCO and x%GL/S1 (x = 5, 10, 15, 20) heterojunctions for TC (50 mg/L) under simulated sunlight irradiation. After 30 min of adsorption, all the photocatalysts reached adsorption equilibrium, and the adsorption percentage was less than 15 %, indicating that all the photocatalysts had weak adsorption capacity. The degradation rate of all photocatalysts increased with the increasing of irradiation time after the occurrence of light illumination. With the introduction of GL, the photocatalytic activity of x%GL/S1 heterojunction increases significantly, which is due to the fact that GL, as a medium of electron transport, reduces the recombination efficiency of electron hole pairs and improves the photocatalytic activity [22]. After 30 min photodegradation, the degradation percentage of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction reached the maximum (83.2 %) as shown in Figure S5c. To clarify the photocatalytic degradation rate of x%GL/S1 heterojunction, its first-order kinetic behavior was explored. Fig. 7b shows the pseudo-first-order kinetic curves of NCO and x%GL/S1 (x = 5, 10, 15, 20) heterojunctions for TC degradation. The relationship between ln(At/A0) and t shows a good linear dependence. According to the fitting results of the first-order kinetic rate constant (k), the degradation rates of 5 %GL/S1 (0.02602 min-1), 10 %GL/S1 (0.05152 min-1), 15 %GL/S1 (0.04657 min-1) and 20 %GL/S1 (0.04131 min-1) were pure NCO, respectively 3.56, 7.05, 6.37 and 5.65 times of (0.00731 min-1). The results further confirmed that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction has the best photocatalytic activity for TC degradation. Combined with Figure S5b, it can be seen that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction exhibits higher photocatalytic activity than that of GL, indicating that the GL promotes charge transfer and separation in S1 system. To compare with similar photocatalysts reported by literatures [44,66-69], the relevant results are given in Table 1 . Table 1 shows a comparison of photocatalytic activity of recently reported spinel based or biochar based photocatalysts toward the degradation of TC. According to the calculation of specific activity, the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction prepared in this experiment shows the maximum specific activity value, indicating that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction has great application prospect in the field of photocatalysis. However, a major disadvantage of the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction is that the composition of GL becomes uncontrollable with increasing sintering temperature, which will significantly affect the photocatalytic activity of 10 %GL/S1. Therefore, in the process of synthesizing GL, the temperature must be strictly controlled to obtain the target product with controllable composition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of different catalyst contents
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above results, 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction was selected as the best catalyst to evaluate the effect of catalyst content on photocatalytic activity. All comparison experiments were performed at the same TC concentration (50 mg/L). As shown in Fig. 7c, when the catalyst content increased from 0.5 to 1 g/L, the TC degradation efficiency increased by 20.9 %, which may be due to the large number of catalysts providing more active sites and active free radicals, which improved the photocatalytic activity. When the catalyst content reaches 1.5 and 2 g/L, the degradation efficiency of TC is slightly reduced compared with 1 g/L. This is because the increase in the content of photocatalyst causes the absorbable photons to aggregate on the catalyst surface, reducing the contact area between the catalyst and TC molecules [70]. Figure S4 shows that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction is a dark green photocatalyst. Excessive catalyst will make the TC solution more turbid, weaken the penetration of simulated sunlight, and reduce the photocatalytic activity. [71,72] Therefore, considering the photocatalytic degradation efficiency and economic benefits, 1 g/L was selected as the optimal catalyst content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of different drug concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: Drug concentration is one of the important factors affecting photocatalytic activity. Therefore, 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction was selected as a suitable catalyst (1 g/L) to evaluate the effect of TC concentration on the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst. Fig. 7d demonstrates the degradation percentage of TC solutions in the concentration range of 25–100 mg/L. In the concentration range of 25–50 mg/L, the degradation percentage of TC increased significantly with the gradual increasing of drug concentration. When the TC concentration increased from 50 to 100 mg/L, the degradation percentage of TC decreased from 83.2 % to 52.6 %. The reasons are as follows: (1) A high concentration of TC solution will weaken the penetration of simulated sunlight, resulting in a gradual reduction of photons on the catalyst surface [73]. (2) A high concentration of TC solution will reduce the interaction between TC molecules and the catalyst surface [74]. (3) High concentration of TC solution will cover the active site on the catalyst surface, limiting the production of active free radicals [75]. 50 mg/L was selected as the optimal drug concentration for subsequent experiments due to the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction has a highest degradation percentage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of different pH values
ce:section.ce:para:
The research shows that the type of surface charge of the catalyst changes with the change of pH value. This change affects the interaction between the antibiotic and the catalyst, causing the ionic species of the antibiotic molecule to produce a strong repulsive or attractive force [76]. Therefore, it is necessary to study the effect of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction on the percentage of TC degradation at different pHs. As shown in Fig. 7e, when pH values are 3, 5, 6.6, 9 and 11, the corresponding degradation percentages are 61.1 %, 74.7 %, 83.2 %, 84.2 % and 78.4 %, respectively. The degradation efficiency of TC solution is significantly decreased when pHs are 3 and 11. This is because TC will limit the production of CO3 - under strong acid conditions, making the role of GL gradually weakened, reducing the electron transport rate [77]. Under strong acid conditions, nitric acid may react with calcium carbonate in GL to form calcium nitrate, carbon dioxide, and water (Eq. (5)). In the process of this reaction, the degradation efficiency of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction is also inhibited to a certain extent.
(5) 2HNO3 + CaCO3 → Ca(NO3)2 + CO2 + H2O

In addition, higher pH will drive hydroxide to combine with H +, curbing the production of hydroxyl radicals, resulting in a decline in TC degradation efficiency [13]. The results indicated that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction exhibited high photocatalytic activity under neutral and weak base conditions.

The point of zero charge (PZC) was used to evaluate the effect of pH on the surface charge and degradation efficiency of the catalyst. It is well known that TC solutions exhibit different characteristics in different pH ranges. At pH<3.3, TC is positively charged and exists as TCH3+. In the range of 3.3–7.7, TC presents a neutral state (TCH2). At pH above 7.7, TC is negatively charged and has a anionic structure of TCH- [78]. As shown in Fig. 7f, the pHPZC of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction is 7.65, which means that a positive charge is attached to the catalyst surface at pH<7.65. Under acidic conditions (pH = 3), TC appears as a cation with a large amount of positive charge. At this time, the positive charge on the catalyst surface will have a strong electrostatic repulsion with the positive charge in TC, which weakens the interaction between the catalyst surface and TC molecules, and reduces the photocatalytic activity. However, when pH>7.65, the catalyst surface and TC molecules are filled with negative charges. With the gradual increase of pH, the electrostatic repulsion between negative charges becomes more and more obvious, which makes the catalyst lose its function and affects the degradation efficiency of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of different water qualities
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water, deionized water, drinking water and boiled water were selected to evaluate the degradation efficiency of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction for TC degradation in actual water. As shown in Figure S6, compared with deionized water, the degradation efficiency of TC in drinking water and boiled water decreased significantly, reaching 62.4 % and 68.3 %, respectively. It may be due to the presence of special inorganic ions and natural organic matter in drinking water and boiled water, which may react with free radicals in a special way, limiting the role of active free radicals. In tap water, the degradation efficiency of TC solution also decreased slightly. Although the degradation rates of the three different water qualities were significantly reduced, they still maintained relatively good degradation efficiency. These results indicate that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction has excellent application prospects in actual water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic stability and structural stability experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic stability of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction was evaluated by 5 cycles under the same conditions. As shown in Figure S7a, after 5 cycles of experiments, the TC degradation efficiency of 10 %GL/S1 heteropair decreased slightly, from 83.2 % to 75.2 %. This phenomenon may be due to the slight loss of the photocatalyst and the reduction of the active site, which reduces its photocatalytic activity. The results show that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction still has good cyclic stability after 5 cycles. Figure S7b shows the XRD patterns before and after photocatalysis by 10 %GL/S1 system. Except for the change of intensity, the position of the diffraction peaks before and after photocatalysis did not change, indicating that the structure of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction did not change after photocatalysis. In addition, comparison of FTIR spectra before and after photocatalysis can further evaluate the structural stability of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction. As shown in Figure S7c, the characteristic peaks and functional groups of samples before and after photocatalysis did not change significantly, indicating that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction has excellent stability, which is conducive to its application in the field of wastewater treatment. Figure S7d shows the SEM image of after photocatalysis by 10 %GL/S1 system. It can be seen from the figure that the surface morphology of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction after photocatalysis does not change significantly. According to the above experimental results, the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction has high structural stability after photocatalysis, and its phase structure, functional groups and surface morphology do not change significantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical capture experiment and ESR/EPR test

ce:section.ce:para: The free radical trapping experiment can provide a direct analysis of the photocatalytic mechanism. Therefore, 1 mmol/L trapping agent was added to TC solution to study the role of active species in the TC degradation process. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and EDTA-2Na were used as ·OH, ·O2 - and h + trapping agents, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the addition of BQ and EDTA-2Na significantly reduced the degradation efficiency of TC, from 83.2 % to 44.3 % and 46.7 %, respectively, indicating that the h + and ·O2 - both participated in the TC degradation process. It was noted that the introduction of IPA significantly reduced the degradation efficiency of TC, indicating that the ·OH played a crucial role in the photocatalytic process.

There are a large number of inorganic anions such as Cl-, HPO4 -,HCO3 - and SO4 2- in actual water, and these co-existing anions will affect the degradation efficiency of antibiotics to a certain extent. Therefore, 1 mmol/L of NaCl, NaH2PO4, NaHCO3 and Na2SO4 were added into the TC solution as trapping agents to further explore the influence of the above co-existing anions on the degradation efficiency of TC. As shown in Fig. 8b, under 30 min of simulated sunlight irradiation, the decreasing order of TC degradation efficiency is NaCl > Na2SO4 > NaHCO3 > NaH2PO4. The degradation efficiency was 80.2 %, 79.3 %, 78.1 % and 64.6 %, respectively. The presence of anions limits the removal of organic contaminants in AOP because of their ability to occupy active sites on the catalyst surface. In addition, these co-existing anions will consume and trap the highly active free radicals in the photocatalytic system to produce some less active substances, which is not conducive to the removal of organic pollutants. [79,80] It is worth noting that the addition of NaCl has a weak limiting effect on TC, reducing it by only 3 %, possibly due to the competition between Cl- and TC on the 10 %GL/S1 surface. This may be due to the competitive relationship between Cl- and TC on 10 %GL/S1 surface, and it is known by equations (6) that Cl- ions react with ·OH to produce ·ClOH- with low activity. However, NaCl was added in small concentrations and had only a slight inhibitory effect.
(6) ·OH + Cl- → OH- + ·ClOH-

In addition, SO4 2- also affects the degradation efficiency of TC. The reason is that SO4 2- and ·OH formed weak ·SO4 2- (Eq. (7)), which weakened the action of active free radicals to a certain extent.
(7) SO4 2- + ·OH + H + → ·SO4 2- + H2O

With the addition of NaH2PO4, the degradation efficiency of TC decreases significantly, which may be due to a series of reactions between H2PO4 - and ·OH and h + to produce poor active substances (Eqs. (8) and 9) [81]. These active species weakened the role of active free radicals in the photocatalytic process and inhibited the photocatalytic activity of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction. However, the photodegradation efficiency of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction was slightly reduced by the presence of coexisting anions, but a high percentage of degradation was still maintained.
(8) H2PO4 - + ·OH → H2PO4 · + OH-
(9) H2PO4 - + h + → H2PO4 ·

In addition, the presence of hydroxyl radicals can be further verified by adding terephthalic acid during TC degradation. Fig. 8c shows the verification experiment of hydroxyl radical by terephthalic acid. When the reaction solutions obtained at different reaction times were excited with excitation light of 320 nm, an obvious emission peak appeared at 430 nm. As shown in Fig. 8c, the emission peak intensity at 430 nm gradually increases with the increase of illumination time, indicating that hydroxyl radicals exist in the TC degradation process. Notably, the emission peak at 430 nm of the reaction solution was quenched 30 min after adsorption, indicating that no hydroxyl radical was produced during adsorption. The results further confirm that the hydroxyl radical plays an important role in the photocatalytic degradation of TC by 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction.

In addition, oxygen vacancy, as the catalytic active site, can effectively inhibit the recombination of charge carriers and improve the photocatalytic efficiency. Fig. 8d shows the ESR spectrum of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction. The characteristic peak at g = 2.003 indicates that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction contains a large number of oxygen vacancies. This corresponds to the previous analysis of the O 1 s spectrum of XPS. The production of active free radicals was further verified by EPR experiment. DMPO was used as free radical scavenger during the test. As shown in Fig. 8e and f, ·OH and ·O2 - signals are not generated in the dark environment, indicating the importance of simulating sunlight in the photocatalysis process. With the continuous increase of illumination time, four obvious signal peaks appeared in the spectrum of DMPO ·OH. These peaks have a typical intensity of 1: 2: 2: 1, meaning that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction has sufficient redox capacity. Similarly, a strong characteristic signal was observed in the spectrum of DMPO ·O2 -, and its corresponding peak intensity gradually increased with the increasing of illumination time. Thus, the ESR signal intensity was significantly increased under simulated sunlight compared to the dark environment, indicating that the synergistic effect of simulated sunlight with the catalyst can promote the production of more active species.

Table S10 - S17 shows the effects of different photocatalysts, catalyst contents, drug concentrations, pHs, water qualities, capturing agents, co-existing ions for TC degradation process. These data are the results obtained by averaging many repeated experiments, and these results only represent the qualitative analysis results under special experimental conditions. The corresponding laws and trends obtained by the experiments are consistent with the relevant theories of photocatalysts. However, the data from each experiment will change slightly depending on the experimental conditions. The photocatalytic experiments show that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction has high photocatalytic activity for the degradation of TC.

To further study the degradation process of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction for the degradation of TC, high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) was used to analyze photocatalyzed TC and its intermediates. Figure S8 and Fig. 9 show the total spectrum of degradation intermediates and the molecular structure of intermediates, respectively. It can be seen that with the continuous increase of illumination time (10 min), TC molecules gradually decompose into several intermediate products under the attack of free radicals. Among them, 17 major degradation products were named as P17 (m/z = 461), P16 (m/z = 415), P15 (m/z = 437), P14 (m/z = 318), P13 (m/z = 338), P12 (m/z = 302), P11 (m/z = 274), P10 (m/z = 270), P9 (m/z = 279), P8 (m/z = 202), P7 (m/z = 200), P6 (m/z = 166), P5 (m/z = 149) P4 (m/z = 150), P3 (m/z = 102), P2 (m/z = 94), P1 (m/z = 59). Table S18 shows the structural formula of the corresponding intermediate product during TC degradation. The degradation process of TC molecules mainly includes demethylation, deammonification, hydroxylation and a series of ring opening effects (CN bond and benzene ring cleavage). Based on the analysis, a reasonable degradation pathway was obtained. These small molecules form two different degradation pathways as shown in Fig. 10 . For Pathway 1, the dimethylamino group of P18 was attacked by free radicals and underwent demethylation to form P16 due to low bond energy of CN [82]. Subsequently, the intermediate P14 is formed through deamination, dehydroxylation and ring-opening effects. P14 is then converted to P12 through oxidation and demethylation [83]. Under the continuous irradiation of simulated sunlight, the active free radicals continuously attack the TC molecules. Meanwhile, P12 undergoes demethylation and dehydroxylation to form intermediates P9 and P10, respectively. P5 and P7 are formed due to further ring opening and dehydroxylation. In Pathway 2, the trapped double bond in the TC (P18) parent body acts as an electron-withdrawing substituent and is vulnerable to free radical attack. The double bond of TC (P18) is deactivated to a hydroxyl group, which is converted to the product P17 [84]. The other double bond in P18 is also prone to hydroxylation. Meanwhile, the double bond in P17 is attacked by ·OH and ·O2 - and undergoes a demethylation reaction to form the intermediate product P15. Under constant attack by •OH and •O2 -, P15 undergoes deamination and cleavage of the carbon ring to form the intermediate P13 [83]. As the oxidation reaction continues, the intermediate P13 further undergoes dehydroxylation to form P11. Under the action of the open-loop effect, the double-carbon bond of P11 is gradually broken and converted into the intermediate P8, and the intermediate P6 is further formed by demethylation. Finally, through a series of ring-opening effects, demethylation, deamination, and oxidation reactions, TC is cleaved into intermediates P1, P2, P3, and P4, which are gradually converted into non-toxic small molecules such as CO2 and H2O. The results show that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction can effectively degrade TC molecules during photocatalysis.

As we all know, tetracycline hydrochloride is a typical antibiotic, but the extensive use of enriched water will pollute the water source, and its ecotoxicity will affect the development of aquaculture industry. Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.) can reasonably assess and detect ecotoxicity of target compounds, providing detailed prediction results [85]. Based on quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR), T.E.S.T. software was used to analyze the toxicity indicators including Fathead minnow LC50 (96 h), Daphnia magna LC50 (48 h), Oral rats LD50, Bioconcentration factor, Deveoplment toxicity and Mutagenicity of TC and its intermediates, and the changes in ecotoxicity of TC and its intermediates were discussed. Specific data were presented in Table S19. As shown in Fig. 11 a, compared with TC (0.9 mg /L), Fathead minnow LC50 (96 h) of most intermediates showed an increasing trend except P7, P10 and P16. In particular, the final products P1, P3 and P4 are “Not harmful”, indicating that the toxicity of most intermediates is gradually reduced after photocatalytic degradation. Fig. 11b shows that the Daphina magna LC50 (48 h) value of TC is 8.73 mg/L, which is shown as “Toxic”. After photocatalytic degradation, Daphina magna LC50 of most intermediates was significantly higher than TC except P2, P5, P7, P10 and P16, and these intermediates were mainly distributed in “Harmful” and “Not harmful”. The results showed that after 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction degradation of TC, the acute toxicity of most intermediates was reduced. The Oral LD50 prediction index of TC (Fig. 11c) also showed that the acute toxicity of most intermediates decreased significantly, especially the end products P3 and P4 showed "Not toxic". As shown in Fig. 11d, it can be clearly observed that the Bioconcentration factor of most intermediates is significantly higher than that of TC, which means that these intermediates still need to undergo sustained photocatalytic degradation [86]. The predicted development toxicity of most degradation intermediates is significantly lower than that of TC except P10, P12 and P16, as shown in Fig. 11e. P4, in particular, is thought to development non-toxicity. The results show that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction can effectively degrade TC, and the ecotoxicity of the degradation products is gradually reduced to non-toxicity. Since the mutagenic of TC (Fig. 11f) was "Mutagenic positive" (>0.5), it implied that TC had a high mutagenesis risk. However, some intermediates were “Mutagenic negative”, indicating that the risk of mutagenicity of most intermediates was gradually reduced under the attack of active radicals. In particular, the final products P2 and P4 are “Mutagenic negative”. The above toxicity prediction results indicate that TC can produce many less toxic intermediates after photocatalytic degradation. However, there are still a few intermediates with strong ecotoxicity. These intermediates are still converted to less toxic intermediates through peroxidation and continuous photocatalytic degradation until they are nontoxic. In summary, 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction can effectively degrade TC, which is conducive to the toxic degradation of water resources and the development of aquaculture.

Based on the results of toxicity analysis, the rationality and feasibility of TC degradation by 10 %GL/S1 heterocombination was further verified by recording the germination degree of mung bean seeds within 6 days. Fig. 12 shows the germination degree of mung bean under three different water conditions. The results showed that mung beans in deionized water could grow normally within 6 days. In contrast, in the TC solution (50 mg/L), only a small portion of mung beans can achieve germination. It is worth noting that TC solution after photocatalytic degradation can provide an environment suitable for seed growth, indicating that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction can effectively reduce the toxicity of TC solution and help mung bean germination. In addition, Figure S9 shows the germination length of mung beans under different water quality. In deionized water, the maximum length of sprouts can reach 8.5 cm. In TC solution, the average length of bean sprouts was only about 3 cm, indicating that TC solution had high toxicity. Fortunately, the sprouts were able to grow normally (8.3 cm) in degraded TC solution, further demonstrating that the 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction can effectively reduce the ecotoxicity of TC solution. Seed germination was more easily observed with 25 mg/L TC solution due to the pH of 25 mg/L TC solution being easier to inhibit the growth of sprouts. As shown in Figure S10 (a)-(c), mung bean seeds could not germinate in a TC solution with an initial concentration of 25 mg/L. However, TC solution after photocatalytic degradation can provide a comfortable growth environment for mung beans, so that the length of bean sprouts can reach 25 cm.

A novel NAO/NCO heterojunction photocatalyst was constructed by low temperature sintering technique. Subsequently, GL is introduced in the NAO/NCO to construct a 10 %GL/S1 ternary heterojunction. Under the action of GL, electrons in NAO/NCO can be transferred to the surface of GL, effectively impeding the recombination rate of electron hole pairs. In addition, these electrons are able to participate in the reaction to produce more active species, thus improving photocatalytic activity of NAO/NCO heterojunction. The photocatalytic mechanism of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction is proposed by combining band arrangement theory, XPS electron transfer mechanism, capture experiment and work function information.

Firstly, the charge transfer mechanism of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction is discussed and explained by the band arrangement theory of semiconductor. Previous studies have calculated in detail the conduction and valency band values of NAO to −0.48 and 2.44 V, respectively [55]. In this work, the conduction band and valence band potentials of NCO calculated by Eqs. (10)-(12) are 0.16 and 1.93 V, respectively.

Where, X is the absolute electronegativity of a pure semiconductor, Ee is the energy of a free electron at a standard hydrogen potential (4.5 eV), and Eg is the band gap value calculated by the Tauc formula.

Based on the energy band structure of NAO and NCO, a possible mechanism of heterojunction photocatalysis is proposed. As shown in Figure S11, both NAO and NCO maintain a stable energy before the semiconductor material contact. Under simulated sunlight irradiation, photoinduced electrons are transferred from the conduction band of NAO to the conduction band of NCO, and photogenerated holes are transferred from the valence band of NAO to the valence band of NCO. The electron transfer mechanism conforms to the typical type I heterojunction structure. The valence band potential of NCO is smaller than the reduction potential of H2O/•OH (2.38 V vs NHE), so h + in the valence band cannot react with H2O to form hydroxyl radical (•OH). Similarly, the conduction potential of NCO is much greater than the reduction potential of dissolved oxygen (E (O2/•O2 -) = −0.33 V vs NHE), [87-89] resulting in the inability of electrons in the conduction band of NCO to form superoxide radicals with O2 (•O2 -). In conclusion, the transport mechanism of type I heterojunction can accelerate the separation efficiency of electron hole pairs to a certain extent and improve the photocatalytic activity under the action of built-in electric field. However, neither •OH nor •O2 - played any role in the photocatalyst mechanism, resulting in the photocatalytic efficiency did not reach the optimal effect. In this experiment, the photocatalytic activity of NAO/NCO heterojunction degradation of TC is weak, indicating that the type I band arranged NAO/NCO heterojunction is easy to recombine electron hole pairs, so it is necessary to introduce a carrier that can separate its charge carriers to enhance the photocatalytic activity of the system.

Surface work function is an important parameter of semiconductor materials, which helps to explain the charge transfer mechanism of semiconductor interface, so combining DFT calculation provides a more reasonable explanation for the charge transfer mechanism of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction and photocatalysis mechanism. As shown in Fig. 13 a and b, according to the optimized crystal structure, the work functions of NAO and NCO are calculated to be 5.486 and 4.546 eV, respectively. In the preparation process, a typical type I heterostructure was formed between NAO and NCO. At this point, the Fermi level of the NCO is much higher than that of the NAO, causing free electrons to diffuse from the NCO into the NAO until the Ef is in an equilibrium state. A large amount of negative charge is generated on the surface of NAO due to the large work function of NAO. On the contrary, there is a large amount of positive charge on the surface of NCO. Under the action of positive and negative charges, an internal electric field (IEF) is generated inside the interface of NAO and NCO. In IEF, the conduction and valence bands of NCO bend upward, while the conduction and valence bands of NAO bend downward. At this time, the electrons in the NAO conduction band are transferred to the NCO. Because of the bending of the valence band, the holes in the valence band of NAO cannot be transferred to the valence band of NCO. Compared with the transport mechanism of type I heterojunction, the valence band of NAO is greater than the reduction potential of H2O/•OH (2.38 V vs NHE). Therefore, h + in the valence band can react with H2O to form hydroxyl radical, which is conducive to the degradation of TC.

In the process of photocatalysis, biological carbon-based materials can be used as a bridge of electron transport, effectively inhibiting the recombination efficiency of electrons and hole pairs, and improving the photocatalytic activity [90,91]. The results of C element mapping image show that GL contains a large number of C elements. Therefore, the introduction of GL into S1 can effectively transfer photogenerated electrons and inhibit the recombination of electron hole pairs. In addition, with the addition of GL, a large number of active sites are generated on the surface of S1, which is conducive to the redox reaction to produce a large number of active species and improve the photocatalytic activity. Specifically, a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups transfer electrons from the NCO conduction band to the GL surface. Meanwhile, the electrons on the surface undergo a redox reaction to produce a large amount of •O2 -, which further interacts with water to produce •OH [92,93]. In addition, capture experiments and ESR test results show that 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction contains a large number of active free radicals, including •OH, •O2 - and h +. These results correspond to the above analysis and further verify the rationality of the photocatalytic mechanism.

A possible electron transport mechanism based on XPS spectra is obtained. When GL is combined with S1, the fitting peaks of Ni 2p, Cr 2p and O 1 s in S1 show a slight blue shift. In contrast, the P 2p and S 2p spectra of GL show a slight redshift, indicating that electrons may be transferred from S1 to GL. This corresponds to the electron transfer process mentioned earlier, and further shows that the introduction of GL can induce electron transfer, which is conducive to the type I heterojunction to better restrain the generation of electron hole pairs. In addition, the introduction of GL makes these surface electrons generate a large number of active species after oxidation reaction, which improves the photocatalytic activity of S1 binary heterojunction. In summary, the photocatalytic mechanism of 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction was plotted, as shown in Fig. 13c. The specific reaction process is described in Eqs. (13)-(23).
(13) NAO + hv→e - NAO+h + NAO
(14) NCO + hv →e - NCO+h+ NCO
(15)
h + NAO + H2O→•OH +H+
(16)
e - NAO + e - NCO →e - GL
(17)
e - GL + O2 →•O2 -
(18) •O2 - + H+→ •HO2
(19) •HO2→ H2O2 +O2
(20) H2O2 + •O2 - →OH- +O2 + •OH
(21)
h + NAO + TC →CO2 +H2O + non-toxic product
(22) •O2 -+ TC→ CO2 +H2O + non-toxic product
(23) •OH + TC →CO2 +H2O + non-toxic product

High performance NAO/NCO/GL heterojunction photocatalyst was prepared by the polyacrylamide gel method combined with low temperature sintering technology. The experimental results showed that the photocatalytic activity of NAO/NCO heterojunctions was effectively improved by introducing GL into NAO/NCO, and the degradation rate of TC reached 83.2 % under simulated sunlight irradiation (30 min). On the one hand, the introduction of biochar based materials (GL) effectively transfers photogenerated electrons in heterojunctions, weakening the recombination efficiency of electrons and holes in NAO/NCO. On the other hand, there are a large number of active sites on the surface of GL, which is conducive to enhancing the interaction between TC and the catalyst. In addition, the addition of GL can produce a large number of active free radicals, which can continuously attack TC molecules. HPLC-MS and toxicity analysis confirmed that after simulated sunlight irradiation, the toxicity of TC and its intermediates gradually decreased until they showed no toxicity. After 5 cycles, 10 %GL/S1 heterojunction still has good degradation efficiency, indicating that the catalyst has excellent cyclic stability. Therefore, the heterojunction photocatalyst prepared in this study provides a new measure for the field of wastewater treatment, and also provides a new idea for the application of biochar based materials in the field of photocatalysis.
